hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_title: Small Talk That Works -- Starting, Sustaining, and Exiting Casual Conversations
lesson.menu_desc: The cocktail reception at the trade fair in Lyon: how to walk up to a stranger, open with an icebreaker, find common ground, keep it going with a follow-up question, and exit gracefully -- 'It was great meeting you, let\'s stay in touch.' New words for the room (small talk, icebreaker, common ground, networking, reception, to mingle, follow-up question, to wrap up) and the grammar that connects your ideas: relative pronouns (who, which, that, where) -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 15 -- Small Talk That Works
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 15 -- Small Talk That Works

listenings.text: Welcome to your first big trade fair. Let me give you one piece of advice that took me years to learn. The people who do well here are not the ones with the best products. They are the ones who are easiest to talk to. So when you walk into the reception tonight, do not rush to sell anything. Find some common ground first. Ask a simple question that opens the door, something like, what brings you here, or, have you been to this fair before. Those are the icebreakers that make a stranger relax. And then listen, really listen, because a good follow-up question is worth more than a business card. When you meet someone whose company does something close to yours, that is the person you want to catch up with later. Keep it lighthearted, keep it short, and when it is time to move on, just say, it was great meeting you, let us stay in touch. That is how you wrap up a conversation without being rude. Then you mingle, and you do it again. That is the whole game.

Hi Lucimara, it is Camille from the Lyon office. I just wanted to give you a quick heads up about the reception tonight, because I know it is your first one. It starts at seven, in the main hall, which is the big room where they had the opening ceremony this morning. Do not worry about knowing anyone. Nobody does at first, and that is the whole point of the evening. There is a woman named Sofia who runs the cosmetics pavilion. She is the person who I told you about on the phone, and she is really easy to talk to, so I will introduce you. My one tip is to bring plenty of cards, because you will exchange them all night. And if the small talk ever feels hard, just ask people about the city. Lyon is a place where everyone has an opinion about food, and that always breaks the ice. See you at seven. Look for me near the entrance.

items.situation: You walk up to someone standing alone at the reception. Break the ice with a friendly opener.
tips: Hi, I don't think we've met. What brings you here tonight?
'What brings you here?' is the classic icebreaker -- open, friendly, and it hands the conversation to the other person.

items.situation: The person tells you they work in cosmetics. Find common ground with your own work.
tips: Oh, that's close to me -- I work for a company that makes ingredients for personal care products.
A relative pronoun ('that makes ingredients') lets you describe your company in one smooth line, not two choppy sentences.

items.situation: The conversation is going well. Keep it alive with a follow-up question.
tips: Have you been to Lyon before? It's a city where the food is unforgettable.
A follow-up question keeps the ball in the air -- and 'a city where...' shows off the grammar of tonight.

items.situation: You want to introduce the colleague who helped you last year. Point them out.
tips: That's Paulo, the colleague who introduced me to our supplier in China.
'who' for people: the colleague WHO introduced me -- never 'which' for a person.

items.situation: The conversation is slowing down and you'd like to move on. Exit politely.
tips: It was great meeting you -- let's stay in touch. I'll go and mingle a little.
'It was great meeting you, let's stay in touch' is the graceful exit -- warm, short, and it leaves the door open.

items.situation: Someone asks what your company does. Answer in one sentence, using a relative pronoun.
tips: We're a company that produces chemical ingredients which go into shampoos and creams.
Two relative pronouns in one line -- 'that produces' and 'which go into' -- and you sound fluent, not rehearsed.
